flashcards.front: What is the primary purpose of Controlled Access Protocols in data communication?
flashcards.back: To regulate access to a shared communication channel by requiring nodes to obtain explicit permission before transmitting, thereby preventing data collisions and ensuring orderly data transmission.
A controlled access method where a special frame called a 'token' circulates among nodes. Only the node possessing the token has the right to transmit data, ensuring mutual exclusion.

flashcards.front: What are the two main types of Token Passing networks?
flashcards.back: Token Ring (IEEE 802.5) - nodes connected in a logical ring topology; Token Bus (IEEE 802.4) - nodes connected in a bus/linear topology but form a logical ring for token passing.

flashcards.front: What is polling in controlled access protocols?
flashcards.back: A central controller (primary node) sequentially invites each secondary node to transmit by sending a poll frame. Only the polled node can send data.

flashcards.front: What happens when a token is lost in a Token Ring network?
flashcards.back: A special monitor node detects token loss and initiates a claim token process. A new token is generated after a specified timeout period to restore network operation.
The maximum time a node can hold the token and transmit data. It is typically limited to prevent any single node from monopolizing the channel.

flashcards.front: What is the difference between Token Ring and Token Bus?
flashcards.back: Token Ring uses physical ring topology with tokens circulating directly; Token Bus uses physical bus topology but implements a logical ring using MAC addresses for token circulation.
A controlled access method where nodes must reserve time slots before transmitting. Used in satellite networks and wireless systems where nodes can reserve slots for future transmissions.

flashcards.front: What are the three states of a node in Token Ring?
flashcards.back: 1) Monitor state (oversees token); 2) Claim state (for token regeneration); 3) Transmit state (when holding token); 4) Receive state (when receiving data).

flashcards.front: What is the maximum token holding time in Token Ring?
flashcards.back: Typically 10 milliseconds (ms), though it can be configured. This ensures fair access to all nodes on the network.
The time taken for the token to travel from one node to the next in the ring. It is crucial in large networks as it affects overall network efficiency.

flashcards.front: What is a priority system in Token Ring?
flashcards.back: A mechanism where nodes can request priority for token access. The token carries a priority field; only nodes with equal or higher priority can seize the token.

flashcards.front: What is the function of the Active Monitor in Token Ring?
flashcards.back: The Active Monitor (typically one of the nodes) monitors network operation, detects lost tokens, generates new tokens, and maintains synchronization.

flashcards.front: How does polling differ from token passing?
flashcards.back: Polling uses a central controller that explicitly invites each node to transmit; token passing uses a distributed approach where the token circulates autonomously among nodes.

flashcards.front: What is the main advantage of controlled access over random access protocols?
flashcards.back: Controlled access guarantees no collisions (collision-free), predictable latency, and fair channel allocation, making it suitable for real-time and mission-critical applications.
